title: 第 17 節
en: ‘In a very large number of legends [he says], the parents, warned that their own offspring will destroy them, expose their children, who are saved by some wild beast and brought up by some herdsman. The children so recovered always grow up beautiful, brave, strong, and generous; but, either unconsciously or 170 GREEK LEGENDS.

against their will, they fulfil the warnings given before their birth, and become the destroyers of their parents. Perseus, G:dipus, Cyrus, Romulus, Paris, are all exposed as infants, are all saved from death, and discovered by the splendour of their countenances and the dignity of their bearing. Either consciously or unconsciously Perseus kills Akrisios, (:dipus kills Laios, Cyrus kills Astyages, Romulus kills Amulius, and Paris brings about the ruin of Priam and the city of Troy.’

Mr. Cox supposes that all these names are solar names, and that the mythical history of every one of these heroes is but a disguise of language. Originally there must have existed in ancient languages a large number of names for the sun, and the sky, and the dawn, and the earth. The vernal sun returning with fresh vigour after the deathlike repose of winter had a different name from the sun of summer and autumn; and the setting sun with its fading brilliancy was addressed differently from the ‘ bridegroom coming forth out of his chamber,’ or ‘the giant rejoicing to run his course.’ Certain names, expressions, and phrases sprang up, originally intended to describe the changes of the day and the sensons of the year; after a time these phrases became traditional, idiomatic, proverbial; they ceased to be litcrally understood, and were misunderstood and misinterpreted into mythical phraseology. At first the phrase ‘Perseus will kill Akrisios’ meant no more than that light will conquer darkness, that the sun will annihilate the night, that the morn is coming, If each day was called the child of the nicht, it might be truly said that the young child was destined to kill its parents, that Cedipus must GREEK LEGENDS. 171 kill Laios?. And if the violet twilight, Iokaste, was called the wife of the nocturnal Laios, the same name of Iokaste, as the violet dawn, might be given to the wife of Cdipus. Hence that strangely entangled skein of mythological sayings which poets and philosophers sought to disentangle as well as they could, and which at last was woven into that extraordinary veil of horrors which covers the sanctuary of Greek religion.

. But if this be so—and, strange as it may sound at. first, the evidence brought in support of this inter- 3 Professor Comparetti, in his Essay ‘ Edipo e la Mitologia Comparata’ (Pisa, 1867), has endeavoured to combat M. Bréal’s explanation of the mythe of Cidipus. His arguments are most carefully chosen, and supported by much learning and ingenuity which even those, who are not convinced by his able pleading, cannot fail to appreciate. It is not for me to defend the whole theory proposed by M. Bréal in his ‘Mythe d’Cedipe’ (Paris, 1863). But as Professor Comparetti, in controverting the identification of Laios with the Sanskrit disa or dasya, denies the possibility of an Aryan d appearing in Greek as |, I may, in defence of my own identification of disahanta with Aewdorrn¢e (Kuhn’s ‘ Zeitschrift,’ vol. v. p. 152), be allowed to remark that I had supported the change of d into ] in Greek by instances taken from Ahrens, ‘ De Dialecto Dorica,’ p. 85, such as Adgrn=cagrn, Odvecevg='Ucvacere, and Nioxoc=Cisxoc. If in any of the local dialects of Greece the dental media could assume the sound of 1, the admission of the change of a Greek d into a Greek | was justified for the purpose of explaining the name of one or two among the local heroes of ancient Greece, though I grant that it might be open to objections if admitted in the explanation of ordinary Greek words, such as Aao¢ or peAeraw. If therefore Professor Curtius (‘ Grundziige der Griechischen Etymologie,’ p. 325) calls the transition of d into ] unheard of in Greek, he could only have meant the classical Greek, and not the Greek dialects, which are nevertheless of the greatest importance in the interpretation of the names of local gods and heroes, and in the explanation of local legends.

pretation of mythology is irresistible—it would seem to follow that Perseus, and C:dipus, and Paris, and Romulus could none of them claim any historical reality. Most historians might be prepared to give up Perseus, (Edipus, and Paris, perhaps even Romulus and Remus; but what about Cyrus? Cyrus, like the other solar heroes, is known to be a fatal child; he is exposed, he is saved, and suckled, and recognised, and restored to his royal dignity, and by slaying Astyages he fulfils the solar prophecy as completely as any one of his compeers. Yet, for all that, Cyrus was a real man, an historical character, whose flesh and bone no sublimating process will destroy. Here then we see that mythology does not always create its own heroes, but that it lays hold of real history, and coils itself round it so closely that it is difficult, nay, almost impossible, to separate the ivy from the oak, or the lichen from the granite to which it clings. And here is a lesson which comparative mythologists ought not to neglect. They are naturally bent on explaining everything that can be explained; but they should bear in mind that there may be elements in every mythological riddle which resist etymological analysis, for the simple reason that their origin was not etymological, but historical. The name of Cyrus or Koresh has been supposed to have some affinity with the Persian name of the sun, khvar or khor; and, though this is wrong, it can hardly be doubted that the name of Astyages, the Median king, the enemy of Cyrus, doomed to destruction by a solar prophecy, is but a corruption of the Zend name Azhi dahaka, the destructive serpent, the offspring of Ahriman, who was chained by Thraétaona, and is to be killed at the end of days GREEK LEGENDS, 173 by Keresispa. Mr. Cox refers several times to this Azhi dah&ka and his conqueror Thraétaona, and he mentions the brilliant discovery of Eugéne Burnouf, who recognised in the struggle between Thraétaona and Azhi dah&ka the more famous struggle celebrated by Firdusi in the Shahnameh between Feridun and Zohak®. If, then, the Vedic Ahi, the serpent of darkness destroyed by Trita, Indra, and other solar heroes, is but a mythological name, and if the same applies to Azhi dah&ka, conquered by Thraétaona, and to the Echidna slain by Phebus, and to Fafnir slain by Sigurd, what shall we say of Astyages killed by Cyrus? We refer those who take an interest in these questions to’a posthumous work of one of the most learned dignitaries of the Roman Catholic Church, the ‘ Zoroastrische Studien’ of F. Windischmann. The historical character of Cyrus can hardly be doubted by any one, but the question whether _Astyages was assigned to him as his grandfather merely by the agency of popular songs, or whether Astyages too was a real king, involves very important issues, particularly as, according to Windischmann, there can be no doubt as to the identity of Darius, the Median, of the Book of Daniel, and Astyages. What is called the history of Media before the time of Cyrus is most likely nothing but the echo of ancient mythology repeated by popular ballads. Moses of Khorene distinctly appeals to popular songs which told of Ajdahak, the serpent’, and, with regard to the changes of the name, Modjmil? says that the Persians 3 See ‘Essay on the Zend- Avesta,’ vol. i. p. 98. 4 Windischmann, ‘Zoroastrische Studien,’ p. 138. 5 ‘ Journal Asiatique,’ vol. xi. p. 156.

gave to Zohak the name of Dehak, i.e. ten evils, because he introduced ten evils into the world. In Arabic his name is said to have been Dechak, the laugher, while his other name Azdehak is explained as referring to the disease of his shoulders, where two serpents grew up which destroyed men®. All this is popular mythology, arising from a misunderstanding of the old name, Azhi dah&ka; and we should probably not be wrong in supposing that even Dejoces was a corruption of Dehak, another ancestor in that Median dynasty which came to an end in Astyages, the reputed grandfather of Cyrus. We can here only point to the problem as a warning to comparative mythologists, and remind them, in parting, that as many of the old German legends were transferred to the Apostles, as some of the ancient heathen prophecies were applied to the emperor Barbarossa, as tricks performed by solar archers were told again of a William Tell, and Robin Hood, and Friar Tuck—nay, as certain ancient legends are now told in Germany of Frederick the Great—it does not always follow that heroes of old who performed what may be called solar feats are therefore nothing but mythes. We ought to be prepared, even in the legends of Herakles or Meleagros or Theseus, to find some grains of local history on which the sharpest tools of comparative mythology must bend or break.
Mareh, 180¢.
© Windischmann. |. c. p. 37.

HAT was the original intention of the name of Bellerophon? That bellero, the first part of the word, represents some power of darkness, drought, cold, winter, or of moral evil, is easy to guess. The Greeks say that there was a word re éaagoa, which signified anything evil or hateful}, and was used in that sense by Kallimachos’. Nay, Bellerophon or Bellerophontes is said to have been called also Ellerophontes. That the Greeks in general, however, were no longer conscious of the appellative power of Belleros, is best proved by the fact that, in order to explain the mythe of Bellerophon, they invented, very late, it would seem, a legend, according to which Bellerophon had killed a distinguished Corinthian, of the name of Belleros, and had fled to Argos or Tyrins to be purified by Preetos from the stain of that murder. Nothing, however, is known about this Belleros, and as the ordinary accounts represent Bellerophon as flying to Argos after having killed his brother Deliades, or, as he is also called, Peiren or Alkimenes, there can be little doubt that the Corinthian nobleman of the 1 Preller, ‘Griechische Mythologie,’ vol. ii. p. 55. 3 Eustath. ad Il. p. 635; Naeke, ‘Opuse.’ vol. ii. p. 167.
name of Belleros owes his origin entirely to a desire of later mythologists, who felt bound to explain the no longer intelligible name of Bellerophon or Bellerophontes.

Such a name, it is quite clear, was not originally without some meaning, and without attempting to unravel the whole tragedy of Hipponoos, who afterwards monopolised the name of Bellerophon, it may be possible to discover by a strict observance of etymological laws, the original form and the original purport of this peculiar name.

With regard to the second half of the name, there can be little doubt that in Bellerophon and Bellerophontes, phén and phontés had one and the same meaning. Now phon-tés at the end of compounds means the killer, the Sanskrit han-t4, killer; and therefore phén can, in our name, hardly mean anything else, and would correspond exactly with the Sanskrit han, nom. hé4, killing.

From the reported change in the initial letter of Bellerophon, it is easy to see that it represents a labial liquid, and is in fact the well-known digamma AXolicum, But it is more difficult to determine what letters we ought to look for as corresponding in other languages to the aa of the Greek word bellero. In many cases Greek aa represents a single 1, followed originally by a sibilant or a liquid®. In this manner we can account for the single 1 in weAus and the double 1 in x0%A0i. [Toaug corresponds to the Sanskrit pult (Rv. I. 179, 5), or purd, gen. puros, whereas the oblique cases would represent a Sanskrit adjective purva, gen. pirvdsya. As woaao points to a > See Ahrens, ‘ Dial. Dor.’ p. 60.

BELLEROPHON. 177 Sanskrit purvé, odo points to the Sanskrit sarve. In Latin, too, a double 1 owes its origin not unfrequently to an original single | or r followed by v*. Thus the double | in mellis, the gen. of mel, honey, is explained by the Sanskrit madhu, raised to madhv-i, and regularly changed to madv-i, malv-i, mall-i. Fel, gen. fellis, is explained by haru in haru-spex’, raised to harv-i, halv-i, hall-i, fall-i8. Mollis corresponds to Sanskrit mridu, through the intermediate links, mardv-i, maldv-i, malv-i, mall-i’; nay, if we consider the Vedic word for bee, ridu-p& (Rv. VIII. 77, 11), mel, mellis, too, might be derived from mridu, and not from madhu. According to these analogies, then, the Greek féaaspo would lead us back to a Sanskrit word varvara. This word actually occurs in the Sanskrit language, and means hairy, woolly, shaggy, rough. It is applied to the negro-like aboriginal inhabitants of India who were conquered and driven back by Aryan conquerors, and it has been identified with the Greek BapBazes. Sandal-wood, for instance, which grows chiefly on the Malabar coast, is called in Sanskrit barbarottha, sprung up among Barbaras, because that coast was always held by Tamulian or non-Aryan people. Professor Kuhn, identifying barbara and BapPagoc, refers the meaning of both words, not to the shaggy or woolly hair, but to the confused speech (balbutire) of. non-Aryan 4 Corssen, ‘ Kritische Beitriige,’ p. 385.
5 Aufrecht, in Kuhn’s ‘ Zeitschrift,’ vol. iii. p. 198.
6 As to the interchange of h and f in Latin, see Corasen, ‘Kritische Beitrige,’ p. 208; as to the etymology of fel, ibid. p. 318.
7 Corssen, ‘ Kritische Beitriige,’ p. 323.

tribes. It will be difficult to prove with what intent the Greeks and the Hindus first applied PagPazes and barbara to tribes differing from themselves both in speech and aspect. It is true that in Greek the word occurs for the first time in Homer with a special reference to language (‘ Iliad,’ il. 876, Kazeg BazBacchwvo); and in Sanskrit also the earliest passage in which barbara is found, refers to speech (Rig-veda Pratisikhya, Sitra 784; XIV. 6). But the barbarataé there mentioned as a fault of pronunciation, is explained by the same word (asaukumfryam) which in Satra 778 serves as an explunation of lomasya; and thislomasya, meaning shagginess, is, like the Greek éacurys, clearly transferred from the shagginess of hair (loman, hair), to the shagginess of pronunciation, so that after all, in Sanskrit at least, the original conception of the adjective barbara seems to have been shaggy. However that may be, it is clear that many words for wool are derived from the same root var which yielded varvara or barbara. This root means originally to cover, and it yielded in Sanskrit ura in ura-bhra, ram, 1e. laniger; in Greek elgcs and ec-sov. In the Veda we have likewise the feminine ur, sheep, Rv. VIII. 34, 3, urim nd dhfnute vrikah, ‘(the stone tears the Soma plant) as the wolf tears the sheep.’ The wolf is called urémathi (Rv. VIII. 66, 8), literally the sheep-shaker, or sheep-hifter. From the same root are formed, by means of the suffix na, the Sanskrit Ornf, wool, particularly of sheep; afterwards iirnayu, a goat, and a spider; the one from wearing, growing, or supplying wool; the BELLEROPHON. 179 other from, as it were, spinning or weaving it. Thus the spider is also called in Sanskrit Arna-nabhi and firna-vabhi, literally the wool-weaver; and one of the enemies killed by Indra is Aurnavdbha, which seems to mean a ram rather, a wool-provider, than a spider. This irna, as Bopp has shown, appears again in Russian as vdlna, in Gothic as vulla, r having been changed to ], and In into ll. The same assimilation is found in Latin villus, gen. villi, and vellus, gen. velleris. It might be difficult to convince a classical scholar that vellus was not derived from the Latin vellere, particularly as Varro himself gives that etymology; but it would be equally difficult to establish such an etymology by any analogies. It is curious, however, to remark, for reasons to be explained hereafter, that vellera in Latin signifies light, fleecy clouds. (Virg. Georg. 1, 397; Luc. iv. 124.)

Ura therefore, from a root var, to cover, meant originally cover, then skin, fleece, wool. In its derivatives, too, these various meanings of the root var appear again and again. Thus tiranah means ram, urani, sheep; but urdnah, quite a different formation, means protector. For instance, with the genitive :
Rv. I. 173, 7. samatsu tva sfira satim urfindm pra-pathintamam, ‘Thee, O hero, in battles the protector of the brave, the best guide!’
Rv. VII. 73, 3. dhema yagiiim pathém uriniA, ‘Let us speed the sacrifice, as keepers of the (old) ways!’
With the accusative:
Rv. ITI. 19, 2. (Agnih) deva-tatim urinah, ‘ Agni, who protects the gods.’ Rv. IX. 109, 9. induk punindh pragém uranah, ‘The purified Soma, protecting the people.’
Without any case:
Rv. IV. 6, 4. (Agnih) pra-divah uriindh, ‘ Agni, the old guardian.’ See also Rv. 1V. 7, 3; VI. 63, 4.

Now if urn&, wool, meant originally a covering, var-na also, which now means colour, would seem to have started from the sume conception. Colour might naturally be conceived as the covering, the outside, as ypws and ypmua in Greek combine the meanings of skin and colour. [Irom varna, colour (brightness), we have in Sanskrit varni, gold, as from rapa, form (beauty), we have rfipya, silver, from which Rupee; for we cannot well derive the name of silver, the metal, from the figure (rapa) that was stamped on a silver coin.
In the Veda varna appears in the sense of colour, of bright colour or light, and of race.
In the sense of colour in general, varna occurs, Rv. I. 78, 7. krishnam ka varnam arunim ka sim dhuh, ‘They placed together the dark and the bright colour (of night and day).
Rv. I. 113, 2. dydva varnam karathah a-mininé, ‘ Day and night move on destroying their colour.’
Frequently varza is used in the Veda as implying bright colour or light:
zh: 「在非常多的傳說中，」他說，「父母受到警告，他們自己的子嗣將會摧毀他們，因此暴露（遺棄）他們的孩子，而這些孩子被某種野獸所拯救，並由某位牧人撫養。如此被尋回的孩子總是長大成為美麗、勇敢、強壯且慷慨的人；但是，無論是無意識地或 170 希臘傳說。

違背己意，他們應驗了誕生前所發出的警告，成為了父母的毀滅者。珀爾修斯、俄狄浦斯、居魯士、羅慕路斯、帕里斯，都曾在嬰幼時被遺棄，都從死亡中獲救，並因其容貌的光采與舉止的莊嚴而被發現。無論是有意或無意，珀爾修斯殺死了阿克里西俄斯（Akrisios），俄狄浦斯殺死了萊俄斯（Laios），居魯士殺死了阿斯提亞格斯（Astyages），羅慕路斯殺死了阿木利烏斯（Amulius），而帕里斯則引發了普里阿摩斯及特洛伊城的毀滅。

考克斯先生假定所有這些名字都是太陽名稱，而每一位英雄的神話歷史不過是語言的偽裝。在上古語言中，原本必定存在著大量用以稱呼太陽、天空、曙光和大地的名字。春日的太陽以嶄新的活力從冬日如死亡般的沉寂中歸來，其名字不同於夏秋季節的太陽；落日以褪色的光輝被賦予異於「從他的內室中出來的新郎」或「歡欣於奔跑自己路程的巨人」的稱呼。某些名字、表達方式與短語應運而生，原本是為了描述日間的變化與一年四時的更替；經過時間的推移，這些短語變成了傳統的、習慣用法的、俗語式的；它們不再被字面上理解，而是被誤解和曲解成神話用語。起初「珀爾修斯將殺死阿克里西俄斯」(Perseus will kill Akrisios) 這句話不過是意味著光將戰勝黑暗，太陽將消滅夜晚，黎明將至。若每一個白晝都被稱為夜晚的孩子，便可真誠地說那幼小的孩子註定要殺死其父母，即俄狄浦斯必將殺死拉伊俄斯。而若紫羅蘭色的暮光伊俄卡斯忒被稱為夜間的拉伊俄斯的妻子，那麼同一個伊俄卡斯忒的名字，作為紫羅蘭色的晨曦，也可賦予俄狄浦斯的妻子。因此產生了那奇特而糾纏不清的神話言辭之繚亂，詩人和哲學家曾竭力試圖將其解開，而它最終被編織成覆蓋希臘宗教聖殿的那件非凡的恐怖之幕。

但如果情況確實如此——儘管乍看之下令人詫異，但為支持此一論點所提出的證據卻足以說明³—— ³ 康帕雷蒂教授在其論文《俄狄浦斯與比較神話學》（比薩，1867）中曾試圖反駁布雷亞先生對俄狄浦斯神話的解釋。其論證的選擇極為審慎，並由大量的學識與巧思加以支持，即便那些未為其有力的辯護所說服的人，亦不能不予以讚賞。我無意為布雷亞先生在其《俄狄浦斯神話》（巴黎，1863）中所提出的整個理論進行辯護。但既然康帕雷蒂教授在反駁將拉伊俄斯與梵文 disa 或 dasya 相等同的做法時，否認雅利安語的 d 在希臘語中能出現為 l 的可能性，我得以為自己在《庫恩時刊》第五卷第152頁中將 disahanta 與 Λευδόρροε 相等同的做法辯護，並可被允許注意到：我曾以艾倫斯《多里安方言論》第85頁所引用的例證來支持希臘語中 d 變為 l 的現象，例如 Ἀδμήτη = Ἀλμήτη、Ὀδυσσεύς = Ὀλυσσεύς 和 Νίσκος = Λίσκος。若在希臘的任何地方方言中，齒音濁塞音能夠呈現 l 的音值，那麼為了解釋古代希臘地方英雄中的一、二人名而承認希臘語 d 變為 l 的現象是合理的，儘管我承認若將其應用於普通希臘語詞彙（如 Δάσος 或 μέλεται）的解釋中，則可能招致反對。因此，若庫爾提烏斯教授（《希臘語源學基礎》第325頁）稱 d 變為 l 的過渡在希臘語中是聞所未聞的，他必定只是指古典希臘語而言，而非希臘方言；然而後者對於解釋地方神祇與英雄的名稱，以及對於地方傳說的解釋，具有最高的重要性。

神話學的詮釋是不可抗拒的——因此看來培修斯、俄狄浦斯、帕里斯和羅慕路斯都不能宣稱具有任何歷史實在性。大多數歷史學家可能願意放棄培修斯、俄狄浦斯和帕里斯，甚或羅慕路斯和雷慕斯；但居魯士呢？居魯士如同其他太陽英雄一樣，是已知的命定之子；他遭遺棄、被拯救、被哺育、被識別，並被復位於其王權；通過殺死阿斯提亞格斯，他如同任何同儕一般完整地應驗了太陽預言。然而儘管如此，居魯士卻是真實的人物，是歷史人物，其血肉之軀非任何精化過程所能摧毀。因此，在此我們看到神話學並不總是創造其英雄，而是掌握真實的歷史，並將自身緊緊纏繞其上，使得區分常春藤與橡樹、抑或地衣與其所附著的花崗岩幾乎不可能。這是比較神話學者不應忽視的一課。他們自然傾向於解釋一切可被解釋之物；但他們應當銘記，在每一個神話謎題中可能存在某些元素抗拒詞源分析，原因簡單而言乃是其起源並非詞源學的，而是歷史的。居魯士或寇瑞什（Koresh）之名據謂與波斯太陽之名 khvar 或 khor 有某種親緣性；雖然此說實為謬誤，然而難以否認，米底亞王、居魯士之敵、遭太陽預言所詛咒毀滅的阿斯提亞格斯之名，不過是琐羅亞斯德語名 Azhi dahaka——毀滅性之蛇、阿赫里曼之後裔、為赫拉特阿歐那所鎖鏈、將在末日時由克瑞西斯帕所殺——之訛變而已。考克斯先生數次提及此 Azhi dahaka 及其征服者赫拉特阿歐那，並提及歐仁‧布爾諾夫的傑出發現，該學者在赫拉特阿歐那與 Azhi dahaka 之間的鬥爭中辨識出費爾多西在《沙赫納美》中所慶頌之菲里敦與佐哈克之間更為著名的鬥爭。若然，吠陀的阿希（Ahi）——黑暗之蛇，為特里塔、因陀羅及其他太陽英雄所摧毀——不過是神話學的名字，且若此亦適用於被赫拉特阿歐那征服的 Azhi dahaka、被斐波斯所斬殺的厄喀德那，及被西格蒙德所斬殺的法夫尼爾，我們將作何評斷被居魯士所殺的阿斯提亞格斯？凡對此等問題感興趣者，我們將其介紹給羅馬公教會最博學之高級聖職人員之一的遺著《瑪祖蘭德教義研究》（*Zoroastrische Studien*），該著作由 F. 溫迪施曼所著。居魯士的歷史人物性格難以為任何人所懷疑，然而阿斯提亞格斯是否僅藉民間歌謠之作用而被指派為其祖父，抑或阿斯提亞格斯亦是真實之王，此一問題牽涉甚重大的議題，特別是根據溫迪施曼所言，對於《但以理書》中之米底亞王大流士與阿斯提亞格斯的同一性無法有任何懷疑。所謂居魯士時代之前的米底亞歷史，十之八九不過是被民間民謠反復傳唱的古老神話之迴聲。赫連的摩西明確訴諸民間歌謠，此等歌謠述說阿傑達克（Ajdahak）、蛇，並關於名字之變化，莫季米勒言波斯人。

給了佐哈克（Zohak）德哈克（Dehak）的名字，即「十種罪惡」，因為他將十種罪惡帶入了世界。在阿拉伯語中，他的名字據說是德查克（Dechak），意為「嘲笑者」，而他的另一個名字阿茲德哈克（Azdehak）被解釋為涉及他肩部的疾病，他的肩上長出了兩條蛇，這些蛇摧毀了人類。所有這一切都是民俗神話，源於對古老名字阿茲希達卡（Azhi dahka）的誤解；我們可能不會錯誤地推測，德焦塞斯（Dejoces）本身也是德哈克的訛傳，他是那個以阿斯提亞蓋斯（Astyages）——居魯士（Cyrus）所謂的祖父——為末代君主的米底王朝中的另一位祖先。我們在此只能將這個問題指出作為對比較神話學家的警示，並在此提醒他們，既然許多古老的日耳曼傳說被轉移到了使徒身上，既然某些古代異教預言被應用於巴巴羅薩皇帝，既然由太陽弓箭手執行的把戲被重新講述為威廉‧泰爾（William Tell）、羅賓漢（Robin Hood）和塔克修士（Friar Tuck）的故事，甚至某些古老傳說如今在德國被講述為腓特烈大帝的故事——如果古代英雄執行了所謂的太陽功績，就不必然意味著他們根本不過是神話。我們應當做好準備，即使在赫拉克勒斯（Herakles）、墨勒阿格羅斯（Meleagros）或忒修斯（Theseus）的傳說中，也要發現某些足以使比較神話學的最銳利工具為之折彎或破裂的地方歷史的微粒。
我注意到提供的英文原文僅為「Mareh, 180¢.」，這似乎不是完整的可翻譯內容——可能是掃描文件的頁碼標記或殘缺的片段。 根據您的要求「只輸出翻譯後的繁體中文」，但缺乏實質的英文原文內容，我無法進行翻譯。 請提供完整的英文原文段落，我將按照您列舉的規則為您翻譯成繁體中文。

貝勒洛豐（Bellerophon）這個名字的原始意圖為何？那第一個部分 bellero 代表某種黑暗、乾旱、寒冷、冬季或道德邪惡的力量，是容易猜測的。希臘人說曾有一個詞 re éaagoa，其意義為任何邪惡或令人厭惡的東西，並被卡利馬庫斯（Kallimachos）以此義使用。更且，貝勒洛豐或貝勒洛豐忒斯（Bellerophontes）據稱也曾被稱為艾勒洛豐忒斯（Ellerophontes）。然而，希臘人通常對於 Belleros 一詞的詞源力量已不再有所意識，這最好由以下事實所證明：為了解釋貝勒洛豐的神話，他們很晚才發明了一個傳說，根據該傳說，貝勒洛豐殺死了一位尊貴的科林斯人，名叫貝勒洛斯（Belleros），並逃往阿爾戈斯或提林斯，由普雷托斯（Preetos）為他淨化該謀殺之污漬。然而，關於這位貝勒洛斯卻一無所知，且由於通常的敘述將貝勒洛豐描繪為在殺死其兄弟迪利亞得斯（Deliades），或如他亦被稱呼的珀伊倫（Peiren）或阿爾基墨涅斯（Alkimenes）之後逃往阿爾戈斯，因此幾乎可以肯定那位科林斯貴族
貝勒洛斯（Belleros）這一名字的產生，完全源於後世神話學者的願望，他們感到有必要對不再清晰可解的貝勒洛馮或貝勒洛馮忒斯（Bellerophon or Bellerophontes）這一名字加以說明。
這樣的名字顯然並非原本毫無意義，雖然不必試圖完全揭示後來獨佔貝勒羅封（Bellerophon）之名的希波諾俄斯（Hipponoos）的整樁悲劇，但藉由嚴格遵循語源學的法則，我們仍可能發現這個奇特名字的原初形式與原初涵意。
關於名字的後半部分，對於貝勒洛豐（Bellerophon）和貝勒洛豐特斯（Bellerophontes）中的 phén 和 phontés 具有相同的含義，幾乎沒有疑問。現在 phon-tés 在複合詞的末尾意為「殺害者」，對應於梵文 han-t4（殺害者）；因此 phén 在我們的名字中，幾乎不可能有其他含義，並且將精確地對應於梵文 han（主格：hé4，殺害）。

從貝勒羅豐（Bellerophon）首字母的報告所示變化，不難看出它代表一個脣音液音，實際上就是眾所周知的艾奧利亞雙 Gamma 字 (digamma AXolicum)。然而，要確定在其他語言中應該尋找哪些字母與希臘詞 bellero 的 aa 相對應，則困難得多。在許多情況下，希臘語的 aa 代表一個單個 l，其後原本帶有一個咝擦音或液音。以此方式，我們可以解釋 weAus 中的單個 l 與 x0%A0i 中的雙 l。Toaug 對應於梵文 pult（《梨俱吠陀》I. 179, 5）或 purd、屬格 puros，而該詞的斜格形式則代表梵文形容詞 purva、屬格 pirvdsya。如同 woaao 所指示的一樣⁸ > 見 Ahrens, 'Dial. Dor.' p. 60。

梵文 purvé、odo 指向梵文 sarve。在拉丁文中，雙 l 的起源往往不在少數來自原始單 l 或 r 之後跟隨 v*。因此 mellis（mel 蜂蜜的屬格）中的雙 l，可由梵文 madhu 解釋，提升為 madhv-i，並規則性地變化為 madv-i、malv-i、mall-i。Fel（屬格 fellis）可由 haru 在 haru-spex' 中解釋，提升為 harv-i、halv-i、hall-i、fall-i8。Mollis 對應梵文 mridu，經由中間環節 mardv-i、maldv-i、malv-i、mall-i'；甚至，若我們考慮吠陀中蜜蜂一詞 ridu-p&（《梨俱吠陀》VIII. 77, 11），mel、mellis 亦可能源自 mridu，而非 madhu。據此類比，則希臘文 βέλλαρος 將引領我們回溯至梵文詞彙 varvara。此詞實際上存在於梵文中，意為毛髮蓬亂的、羊毛般的、蓬頭垢面的、粗糙的。它用於指稱被雅利安征服者征服並驅趕的印度類似黑人的土著居民，並已被認定為等同於希臘文 Βάρβαροι。例如檀香木，主要生長於馬拉巴爾海岸，在梵文中被稱為 barbarottha，意為長於蠻族之中，因為該海岸向來由塔米爾人或非雅利安人統治。庫恩教授認定 barbara 與 Βάρβαροι，將兩詞的含義，不歸諸於蓬亂或羊毛般的頭髮，而歸諸於非雅利安人的混雜言語（balbutire）。 > 4 Corssen，《批判性貢獻》，第 385 頁。
5 奧夫萊希特（Aufrecht），刊於庫恩（Kuhn）所編《時代誌》（'Zeitschrift'），第三卷，第 198 頁。
關於拉丁文中 h 與 f 的互換，見科拉森《批判性貢獻》第 208 頁；關於 fel 的語源學，見同書第 318 頁。
7 科森（Corssen），《批判性貢獻》（'Kritische Beitriige'），第323頁。

部落。要證明希臘人和印度人最初何以將 Pagpages 和 barbara 用於在言語和外貌上都與自己相異的部落，將是困難的。誠然，在希臘文中，該詞首次出現於荷馬著作中，特別涉及語言的問題（《伊利亞特》第二卷876行，Kazeg BazBacchwvo）；同樣在梵文中，barbara 最早出現的段落也涉及言語（《梨俱吠陀》準則篇第784部，XIV. 6）。但那裡所述的 barbarataé 作為發音的一個缺陷，乃由同一詞彙（asaukumfryam）來解釋，該詞在第778部用以闡釋 lomasya；而此 lomasya 意為毛茸茸，如同希臘文 éacurys 一樣，顯然是從毛髮的毛茸茸（loman，毛髮）被轉用至發音的毛茸茸，因此到底在梵文中，形容詞 barbara 的原始含義似乎已是毛茸茸的。無論如何，顯而易見，許多表示羊毛的詞彙都衍生自同一個根 var，該根曾產生 varvara 或 barbara。此根原意為覆蓋，它在梵文中產生了 ura，見於 ura-bhra、ram，即 laniger；在希臘文中為 elgcs 和 ec-sov。在吠陀中我們同樣見到陰性詞 ur，綿羊，見《梨俱吠陀》第八卷34詩3節，urim nd dhfnute vrikah，「（石頭撕裂蘇摩植物）如狼撕裂綿羊」。狼被稱為 urémathi（《梨俱吠陀》第八卷66詩8節），字面意為羊的搖撼者或羊的移動者。從同一根衍生而來，藉由後綴 na，有梵文 Ornf，羊毛，特別是綿羊的毛；其後為 iirnayu，山羊和蜘蛛；其一源自穿著、生長或供應羊毛；其他源自於，所謂，紡織或編織它。因此蜘蛛在梵文中也被稱為 Arna-nabhi 和 firna-vabhi，字面意為羊毛編織者；而為因陀羅所殺的其中一個敵人 Aurnavdbha，似乎意為一隻公羊，一個羊毛提供者，勝於蜘蛛。此 irna，如波普所示，在俄文中再次出現為 vdlna，在哥特文中為 vulla，r 已變為 ]，in 變為 ll。同樣的同化作用亦見於拉丁文 villus（屬格 villi）和 vellus（屬格 velleris）。要說服一位古典學者 vellus 不是衍生自拉丁文 vellere，將可能是困難的，特別因為瓦羅本人本身給出了該詞源學；但藉由任何類比來建立如此的詞源學將同樣困難。然而，基於有待於日後說明的理由，值得注意的是，拉丁文 vellera 意為輕盈、羊毛狀的雲彩。（維吉爾《農事詩》第1卷397行；盧坎《內戰記》第四卷124行。）
因此，烏拉（*Ura*）源自詞根 *var*（覆蓋），原本意為「覆蓋」，其後引申為「皮膚」、「羊毛」。在其衍生詞中，詞根 *var* 的這些多種意義也一再出現。例如，*tiranah* 意為「公羊」，*urani* 意為「羊」；而 *urdnah* 則形成方式完全不同，意為「保護者」。舉例而言，以屬格形式：
梨俱吠陀 I. 173, 7. samatsu tva sfira satim urfindm pra-pathintamam，「汝啊，英雄，在戰役中，勇敢者之保護者，最優之嚮導！」
《梨俱吠陀》第七卷第73讚73，第3頌：dhema yagiiim pathém uriniA，「讓我們如同舊道的守護者一樣，加速祭祀！」
帶賓格：
梨俱吠陀 I. 3, 19, 2。(阿格尼) deva-tatim urinah，「阿格尼，保護諸提婆者」。梨俱吠陀 IX. 109, 9。induk punindh pragém uranah，「淨化之蘇摩，保護人民」。
沒有任何格變：
《梨俱吠陀》IV. 6, 4.（阿格尼）pra-divah uriindh，「阿格尼，上古的守護者。」另參見《梨俱吠陀》IV. 7, 3；VI. 63, 4。

現在，如果 urn&、wool 原本意指覆蓋物，那麼 var-na 雖然現在指顏色，似乎也源自同一概念。顏色自然可被理解為覆蓋物、外表，正如希臘文中的 ὑπὸς 和 χρῶμα 兼具皮膚與顏色的含義。[從梵文 varna（顏色、光彩）出發，梵文中有 varni（黃金），正如從 rapa（形態、美）出發，我們得到 rfipya（白銀），由此衍生出盧比（Rupee）；蓋因我們難以從白銀（貨幣上被鑄印的）形態（rapa）來推導白銀這種金屬的名稱。
在《吠陀》中，varna 一詞具有色彩、明亮的色彩或光線，以及種族的含義。
在色彩的一般意義上，*varna* 一詞出現於《梨俱吠陀》卷一‧讚美詩78‧第7頌：krishnam ka varnam arunim ka sim dhuh，意為「他們把黑暗與光亮的色彩（夜晚與白晝）結合在一起」。
《梨俱吠陀》第一卷第113讚歌第2頌：dydva varnam karathah a-mininé，「晝夜運行，摧毀其色彩。」
在《吠陀》中，varza 頻繁被用來表示光亮的顏色或光明：
